title: Topic 185: The Psychology of Gaming Challenges: Self-Imposed Difficulty
content:
## Abstract

This paper delves into the peculiar human behavior of intentionally making leisure activities more difficult, a phenomenon we have dubbed "digital masochism." We explore the psychological drivers that compel gamers to eschew the gentle comforts of "story mode" in favor of the brutal, unforgiving landscapes of self-imposed challenges. Through a rigorous, albeit hilarious, analysis of academic literature and real-world gaming examples, we uncover the reasons behind this seemingly irrational behavior. From the sweet, sweet sting of failure to the glorious, ego-stroking heights of "git gud," we reveal how the pursuit of self-inflicted digital pain is not a sign of madness, but rather a testament to our innate desire for mastery, autonomy, and the right to complain loudly on internet forums. This paper will forever change the way you look at that one friend who insists on playing every game on the hardest difficulty. You will still think they are a little bit crazy, but now you will know why.

## Introduction

The controller flies across the room, a plastic projectile of pure, unadulterated rage. A stream of creative expletives, inventive enough to make a sailor blush, fills the air. The dog, wise to the ways of its human, has already retreated to a safe distance. The cause of this outburst? Not a global catastrophe, not a personal tragedy, but a video game. A video game that the player, in their infinite wisdom, chose to make as difficult as humanly possible. This scene, familiar to many a gamer and their long-suffering loved ones, begs the question: why? Why do we do this to ourselves? Why do we take a form of entertainment, a leisure activity designed for relaxation and enjoyment, and turn it into a crucible of frustration and despair? This paper, dear reader, is dedicated to answering that very question.

We will embark on a journey into the twisted, yet fascinating, psychology of self-imposed difficulty in gaming. We will explore the phenomenon of the "journalist cycle," a term coined to describe the predictable outrage that erupts whenever a game is deemed "too hard" by a member of the gaming press. We will dissect the very fabric of what it means to be a "gamer" in a world where the ability to withstand digital punishment is often seen as a badge of honor. Our thesis is simple: the desire to make games harder is not a sign of a broken psyche, but rather a deeply human need for challenge, a thirst for mastery, and a yearning for the kind of bragging rights that can only be earned through a trial by fire. So, grab your favorite beverage, settle into a comfortable chair, and prepare to understand why your friend’s obsession with “Soulslike” games is less about self-hatred and more about the glorious, triumphant feeling of finally, after the 78th attempt, beating that one ridiculously unfair boss.

## Literature Review

Before we dive headfirst into the masochistic wonderland of self-imposed gaming challenges, let us first consult the dusty tomes of academia. For, believe it or not, psychologists have been studying the very things that make us tick, and by extension, what makes us spend countless hours trying to defeat a single, infuriatingly difficult boss. One of the most prominent theories in this field is **Self-Determination Theory**, proposed by Deci and Ryan. In essence, this theory posits that humans have three innate psychological needs: **autonomy**, the need to feel in control of our own lives; **competence**, the need to feel effective and skillful; and **relatedness**, the need to feel connected to others. Now, how does this relate to our controller-throwing gamer? Well, by choosing to play a game on a harder difficulty, they are exercising their autonomy. They are the masters of their own digital destiny. And when they finally overcome that challenge, they are flooded with a sense of competence that is far more potent than what they would have experienced on an easier setting. As for relatedness, the shared struggle against a difficult game can create a powerful sense of community, a band of brothers and sisters united in their suffering and eventual triumph.

Another key concept is **Flow Theory**, developed by Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi. Flow, often described as being "in the zone," is a state of complete absorption in an activity. It is the magical place where challenge and skill are perfectly balanced, and the outside world fades away. For gamers, flow is the holy grail. It is that moment when their fingers dance across the controller with a will of their own, when they anticipate the enemy's every move, and when time itself seems to bend to their will. Self-imposed challenges are a way of chasing that flow state. By ratcheting up the difficulty, gamers are constantly pushing the boundaries of their skills, ensuring that the challenge is always just high enough to keep them engaged and on the edge of their seats.

Finally, we have the **Paradox of Failure**, a concept eloquently articulated by game designer and scholar Jesper Juul. Juul argues that we are drawn to failure in games because it is what gives success its meaning. A victory that is handed to us on a silver platter is a hollow one. But a victory that is earned through blood, sweat, and a significant number of broken controllers? That is a victory that will be savored, a story that will be told and retold for years to come. In the world of gaming, failure is not the end; it is simply a part of the process, a stepping stone on the path to glory. It is the bitter medicine that makes the sweet taste of victory all the more delicious.

## Analysis

Now that we have a firm grasp of the theoretical underpinnings of our digital masochism, let us dissect the psychological machinations that drive us to embrace the sweet, sweet pain of self-imposed challenges. At the heart of this behavior lies a powerful and often unspoken desire: the “I did it my way” syndrome. In a world where so much of our lives is dictated by external forces, the ability to conquer a virtual world on our own terms is a potent form of self-expression. It is a declaration of independence, a digital middle finger to the notion that we should always take the path of least resistance. The gamer who completes a “Nuzlocke” run in *Pokémon*, a self-imposed challenge with a set of brutally unforgiving rules, is not just playing a game; they are crafting a unique and personal narrative of triumph against overwhelming odds. They are the author of their own heroic saga, a saga that is all the more compelling because of the self-inflicted adversity they have overcome.

This brings us to the crucial concept of **locus of control**. As we learned from our esteemed colleague Dr. Rotter, individuals with a high internal locus of control believe that they are the masters of their own fate. They see success and failure as a direct result of their own actions. For these gamers, a difficult challenge is not a source of frustration, but an opportunity to prove their mettle. When they fail, they do not blame the game; they blame themselves. They see it as a personal failing, a flaw in their strategy or execution that must be corrected. This, in turn, fuels their determination to improve, to “git gud” in the parlance of our times. The phrase “git gud,” which originated in the notoriously difficult *Dark Souls* community, has become a cultural phenomenon in its own right. It is a mantra, a call to arms, and a not-so-subtle jab at those who would dare to complain about a game’s difficulty. It is the verbal embodiment of the internal locus of control, a declaration that the only thing standing between you and victory is your own inadequacy.

Of course, not everyone is blessed with a high internal locus of control. There are those among us who, when faced with a seemingly insurmountable challenge, are quick to point the finger of blame at external forces. The game is “broken,” the controls are “clunky,” the developers are “sadists.” These are the cries of the externalizer, the gamer who cannot or will not accept responsibility for their own failures. And it is this very conflict, this clash between the internalizers and the externalizers, that fuels the never-ending “journalist cycle.” When a game journalist, an individual who is paid to play and critique games, declares a game to be “too hard,” they are often met with a chorus of “git gud” from the internalizers. The journalist, in their view, is simply an externalizer who is unwilling to put in the time and effort required to master the game. The journalist, on the other hand, may see the game as a poorly designed and frustrating experience that is not worth the time and effort. And so the cycle continues, a never-ending debate between those who see difficulty as a challenge to be overcome and those who see it as a barrier to enjoyment.

To illustrate these concepts, let us turn to the hallowed halls of gaming history. The *Dark Souls* series, as we have already mentioned, is the poster child for the “git gud” mentality. Its unforgiving difficulty and cryptic world design have created a cult following of dedicated fans who revel in its challenges. *Cuphead*, with its charming 1930s cartoon aesthetic and brutally difficult boss battles, is another prime example. And then there is *Celeste*, a game that masterfully weaves its narrative about mental health into its challenging platforming gameplay. In *Celeste*, the difficulty is not just a gameplay mechanic; it is a metaphor for the struggles of its protagonist, Madeline. As she climbs the treacherous mountain, she is forced to confront her own inner demons, her anxiety and self-doubt. And in doing so, she learns to overcome them, to “git gud” not just at the game, but at life itself.

## Real-World Examples

To truly appreciate the beautiful absurdity of self-imposed gaming challenges, we must venture into the wild and wonderful world of the gaming community itself. Here, we find a veritable smorgasbord of creative and often bizarre ways in which players have chosen to make their favorite games even more difficult. One of the most well-known examples of this phenomenon is the **Nuzlocke Challenge** in the *Pokémon* series. The rules of a Nuzlocke run are simple, yet brutal: any Pokémon that faints is considered “dead” and must be permanently released. The player can only catch the first Pokémon they encounter in each new area, and all Pokémon must be given a nickname to foster a greater sense of attachment. These simple rules transform a lighthearted children’s game into a heart-wrenching tale of loss and sacrifice. The emotional stakes are raised to an almost unbearable level, and every battle becomes a life-or-death struggle. The Nuzlocke Challenge is a testament to the power of self-imposed rules to completely transform the player’s experience, turning a familiar game into a fresh and challenging adventure.

Then there is the world of **speedrunning**, a subculture dedicated to beating games as quickly as humanly possible. Speedrunners are the masters of optimization, the virtuosos of efficiency. They spend countless hours memorizing patterns, exploiting glitches, and perfecting their techniques, all in the name of shaving a few precious seconds off their personal best. But speedrunning is not just about going fast; it is also about setting and overcoming self-imposed challenges. Many speedrunners will add their own restrictions to their runs, such as completing the game without taking any damage (a “no-hit run”), or using only a specific weapon or character. These self-imposed restrictions add an extra layer of difficulty to an already demanding challenge, and they serve as a way for speedrunners to showcase their skills and creativity. The speedrunning community is a vibrant and supportive one, a place where players can share their strategies, celebrate their achievements, and push each other to new heights of gaming greatness.

Beyond the well-established traditions of Nuzlocke and speedrunning, there is a vast and ever-growing landscape of creative and often hilarious self-imposed challenges. A quick search on Reddit or YouTube will reveal a treasure trove of gaming absurdity. There are players who have beaten *Dark Souls* with a *Rock Band* drum kit, a dance pad, and even a bunch of bananas wired up to a computer. There are players who have completed *The Legend of Zelda: Breath of the Wild* without ever climbing a single mountain. There are even players who have beaten *Super Mario 64* with their feet. These are the true pioneers of self-imposed difficulty, the mad scientists of the gaming world. They are not just playing games; they are pushing the boundaries of what is possible, and they are having a whole lot of fun in the process. Their exploits may seem absurd to the outside observer, but to the gaming community, they are heroes, legends, and a constant source of inspiration and entertainment.

## Discussion

So, what can we glean from this journey into the heart of digital darkness? What does our collective obsession with self-imposed difficulty say about us as a species? For one, it speaks volumes about our innate, and often insatiable, desire for challenge and growth. In a world that increasingly prioritizes comfort and convenience, video games provide a safe and structured environment in which to test our limits, to push ourselves beyond what we thought possible. The act of overcoming a self-imposed challenge, no matter how trivial it may seem in the grand scheme of things, is a powerful affirmation of our own agency and competence. It is a reminder that we are not merely passive consumers of entertainment, but active participants in our own stories of triumph.

For the savvy game developer, this presents a golden opportunity. By understanding the psychological drivers behind the desire for difficulty, they can create more engaging and replayable experiences. The one-size-fits-all approach to difficulty is becoming a relic of the past. The future lies in customizable difficulty options, in giving players the tools to tailor the challenge to their own personal preferences. The rise of community-driven challenges, such as the Nuzlocke run, is a clear indication that players are hungry for more than just what the developers have to offer. They want to be co-creators of their own experiences, to put their own unique spin on the games they love. The developers who embrace this trend, who empower their players to create and share their own challenges, are the ones who will thrive in the years to come.

This also has broader implications for our understanding of consumer behavior. The gamer who willingly subjects themselves to the brutal difficulty of a *Dark Souls* game is, in a sense, the ultimate connoisseur. They are not simply looking for a product that is easy to consume; they are looking for an experience that will challenge them, that will push them to their limits, and that will ultimately reward them with a sense of mastery and accomplishment. This is a far cry from the traditional view of the consumer as a passive recipient of goods and services. The modern consumer, at least in the world of gaming, is an active and engaged participant, a co-creator of value who is willing to work for their enjoyment. This is a lesson that could be applied to a wide range of industries, from education to marketing. The key is to understand that people are not just looking for a product; they are looking for an experience, a challenge, and an opportunity to prove to themselves, and to the world, that they have what it takes to “git gud.”

## Conclusion

In the grand, often bewildering, tapestry of human behavior, the act of intentionally making our leisure time more difficult stands out as a particularly vibrant and perplexing thread. We have journeyed through the psychological landscape of the gamer's mind, from the lofty peaks of Self-Determination Theory to the murky depths of the "journalist cycle." We have seen how the pursuit of self-inflicted digital pain is not a sign of a deeply troubled soul, but rather a testament to our unyielding desire for mastery, our unquenchable thirst for autonomy, and our unwavering commitment to earning the right to say, "I did it my way." So, the next time you see a friend, a loved one, or even a complete stranger, locked in a life-or-death struggle with a virtual dragon of their own choosing, do not pity them. Do not mock them. Instead, offer them a silent nod of respect, a knowing glance that says, "I understand." For they are not just playing a game; they are on a noble quest for self-actualization, one rage-quit at a time. And in the end, is there anything more beautifully, absurdly human than that?
references:
[1] Bornstein, M. H., Hahn, C. S., & Haynes, O. M. (2010). Social Competence, Externalizing, and Internalizing Behavioral Adjustment from Early Childhood through Early Adolescence: Developmental Cascades. *Development and Psychopathology, 22*(4), 717–735.

[2] Deci, E. L., & Ryan, R. M. (2000). The “what” and “why” of goal pursuits: Human needs and the self-determination of behavior. *Psychological Inquiry, 11*, 227-268.

[3] Juul, J. (2013). *The art of failure: An essay on the pain of playing video games*. MIT Press.

[4] Rotter, J. B. (1966). Generalized expectancies for internal versus external control of reinforcement. *Psychological Monographs: General & Applied, 80*(1), 1–28.
